does: This paper describes OpenAI's implementation of safety-by-design principles in response to child safety concerns related to their AI systems. The work outlines a structured approach to embedding child safety considerations throughout the product development lifecycle, from initial design through deployment and monitoring. The paper articulates specific safeguards, content policies, and technical mechanisms designed to protect minors from harmful content and inappropriate interactions. It presents case studies of how these principles were operationalized in particular products and services, including age-appropriate access controls, content filtering systems, and user reporting mechanisms. The document establishes frameworks for identifying child safety risks early in development and integrating mitigation strategies into engineering practices.
connects: This work builds on decades of child protection research and extends principles from broader AI safety and responsible AI development frameworks into the specific domain of child protection. It responds to growing concerns about AI system harms to minors, connecting to prior research on content moderation at scale, detection of child exploitation materials, and age-appropriate design practices from human-computer interaction. The safety-by-design approach builds on established secure development lifecycle methodologies and responsible innovation frameworks used in other high-stakes domains like financial services and healthcare. The paper engages with policy discussions around child online safety regulations and integrates insights from child psychology and developmental stages into technical design choices.
enables: This work enables future development of more nuanced, child-aware AI systems by establishing methodologies for systematically considering developmental differences in AI safety practices. It creates a template for other AI companies to adopt similar child safety frameworks, potentially influencing industry standards and regulatory compliance pathways. The approach enables more sophisticated detection and prevention of child grooming, exploitation, and exposure to harmful content through AI systems. By articulating concrete safety-by-design patterns, the work points toward building child safety considerations into foundation model development from the outset rather than bolting protections on afterward. This sets foundations for multi-stakeholder approaches involving researchers, policymakers, child advocates, and technology companies in establishing shared safeguards for AI systems that minors interact with.
